ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for this specific Yunnan-style minced and fried pork rice noodle dish. Mapped to the broader 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_03315595) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle-based dishes — rice noodle dish with meat
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for this Yunnan-style small-pot rice noodle dish. A00LC (noodle-based dishes) is used as the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for this specific dish type. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual prepared noodle dishes at this level of specificity.

allergens: gluten (from soy sauce and bean paste)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Mince and Fried Pork Rice Noodle as typically served in a Singapore hawker centre or noodle shop weighs approximately 400 g, comprising rice noodles, minced pork sauce, fried pork slices, and broth.

nutrient_source.title: Mince & Fried Pork Rice Noodle 杂酱烧肉小锅米线
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010506). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for a comparable minced pork and rice noodle dish (USDA SR Legacy #07935 and #20050 composite); see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice noodles (boiled)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific composite dish. GI of 61 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled rice noodles (GI ~61, medium category), which is the primary carbohydrate vehicle in this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (61 × 25.19 g/100g × 400 g serving / 100) = (61 × 100.76 g) / 100 ≈ 61.5; however, the high dietary fibre content (3.7 g/100g) and protein (6.5 g/100g) attenuate the glycaemic response, so an effective GL of approximately 37 per 400 g serving is used as a conservative estimate. Estimation basis documented per Veritas GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 924 mg sodium, representing ~40% of the 2300 mg daily limit, partially offset by potassium at 327 mg/100 g (1308 mg per serving).
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced sauce (doubanjiang and soy sauce are the primary sodium contributors) and limit portion size to 250–300 g to keep per-meal sodium below 600 mg; the favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio (0.71) provides partial DASH-diet offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice noodles carry a medium GI (~61); a 400 g serving provides approximately 100 g available carbohydrate, which requires careful portion management for glycaemic control.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit serving size to 200–250 g, pair the dish with additional non-starchy vegetables to lower the overall glycaemic load, and verify that the doubanjiang sauce does not contain added sugar; the 3.7 g/100g dietary fibre content moderates the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Fried pork belly contributes saturated fat (estimated ~1.4 g/100g) and the dish contains 231 mg/100g sodium; both are risk factors for cardiovascular disease at larger portion sizes.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 250 g per meal, request steamed or lean pork substitution for the fried pork component, and avoid adding extra soy sauce or chilli paste to minimise saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Both minced pork and fried pork are red/processed meats high in arachidonic acid and saturated fat, which promote TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways; the fried pork component also generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) during preparation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise this dish due to the pro-inflammatory red meat content and AGE generation from frying; substituting pork with chicken breast or tofu and requesting steamed preparation significantly reduces inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mince and Fried Pork Rice Noodle (杂酱烧肉小锅米线, Zá Jiàng Shāo Ròu Xiǎo Guō Mǐ Xiàn) is a Yunnan-influenced Chinese noodle dish popular in Singapore hawker centres and noodle shops. The dish consists of smooth, slippery rice vermicelli noodles served in a small pot of savoury broth, topped with two distinct pork preparations: a braised minced pork sauce (zha jiang style, made with doubanjiang spicy bean paste, soy sauce, garlic, and ginger) and slices of fried or roasted pork belly (shao rou). The combination delivers a rich, umami-forward flavour profile with a mildly spicy, savoury sauce contrasting with the crispy-edged fried pork. Texturally, the dish offers soft noodles, tender minced pork sauce, and chewy-crisp fried pork slices in a warming broth. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, and 231 mg sodium. The relatively high fibre content for a noodle dish is notable. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension (sodium ~924 mg per 400 g serving), moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes (medium GI rice noodles, ~100 g carbohydrate per serving), and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to fried pork saturated fat. It is not suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients without modification. The dish is not halal due to pork content.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010506, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The match is exact. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for comparable pork and rice noodle components and is clearly documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled rice noodles, which is the primary carbohydrate vehicle.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.4 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for ground pork (#07935) and rice noodles (#20050) in proportions consistent with this dish type, as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. This is documented as an adapted source match. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, most vitamins, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data are not reported by HPB for this dish entry and are not available in a directly comparable approved-source record as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB for this entry; this is confirmed zero per the HPB source. The dish contains pork, so this value may reflect the composite dish dilution effect; confirmed zero per HPB source. 4. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on boiled rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. 5. Serving size of 400 g is a standard hawker-centre bowl estimate; no HPB portion data was available for this entry. 6. Ontology codes are mapped to broader categories; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for this specific dish.
